metadata.description: FAQ Knowledge Base for Astro-AI Galaxy Evolution Analysis Platform

faqs.question: What are the system requirements for installing Astro-AI?
faqs.answer:
System Requirements:
• Python: 3.10 or higher
• Operating System: Linux (recommended), Windows (limited), macOS
• Memory: 4GB RAM minimum, 8GB+ recommended
• Storage: 2GB free space for dependencies

For Streamlit Cloud deployment, the platform uses linux-64 (x86_64 architecture) Debian bookworm containers.

faqs.question: How do I install Astro-AI from scratch?
faqs.answer:
Quick Start (Educational Use Only):

1. Clone the repository:
   git clone https://github.com/Red1-Rahman/CAM-SUST2025.git
   cd CAM-SUST2025

2. Install dependencies:
   pip install -r requirements.txt

3. Launch the application:
   streamlit run app.py

For production deployment with full scientific stack, see DEPLOYMENT.md.

faqs.question: What are the core dependencies for Astro-AI?
faqs.answer:
Core Dependencies:
• Web Framework: Streamlit
• Scientific Computing: NumPy, SciPy, Pandas
• Astronomy: Astropy, Bagpipes
• Visualization: Matplotlib, Seaborn
• AI Integration: OpenAI API

Optional Heavy Dependencies:
• Cosmic Evolution: py21cmfast (for real 21cm simulations)
• Kernel: h5py (HDF5 support)
• JWST: jwst (STScI pipeline)
• Tools: tools21cm (21cm analysis tools)

Use feature_flags.py to detect which optional packages are available.

faqs.question: Why does Bagpipes need a special setup, and how do I configure it?
faqs.answer:
Bagpipes Quirks:
Bagpipes requires BAGPIPES_FILTERS and BAGPIPES_DATA environment variables pointing to valid directories. If these aren't set, Bagpipes initialization fails.

Automated Setup (app.py handles this):
The app automatically creates a writable temporary bagpipes_data directory in the system temp location (e.g., /tmp on Linux/macOS, C:\Users\*\AppData\Local\Temp on Windows) and sets:
• BAGPIPES_FILTERS = <system_temp>/bagpipes_data
• BAGPIPES_DATA = <system_temp>/bagpipes_data
• Mock config module loaded before any bagpipes import

Manual Setup (if needed):
1. Run: python setup_bagpipes.py
2. Or set environment variables before launching:
   export BAGPIPES_DATA=/path/to/bagpipes_data
   export BAGPIPES_FILTERS=/path/to/bagpipes_data

faqs.question: How do I deploy Astro-AI to Streamlit Community Cloud?
faqs.answer:
Streamlit Cloud Deployment Steps:

1. Create a secrets file at .streamlit/secrets.toml with:
   OPENROUTER_API_KEY = "sk-or-v1-..."
   OPENROUTER_MODEL = "deepseek/deepseek-r1:free"

2. Create a packages.txt file listing system dependencies (apt-get installable).

3. Link your GitHub repo to Streamlit Cloud:
   • Go to streamlit.io/cloud
   • Deploy from GitHub
   • Streamlit automatically installs requirements.txt

4. Monitor logs for scientific package installation.

For comprehensive deployment guidance, see DEPLOYMENT.md and FULL_DEPLOYMENT_STRATEGY.md.

faqs.question: What are the main navigation modules in the sidebar?
faqs.answer:
Main Navigation Modules (Select in Sidebar):

1. 🏠 Home - Overview and quick start
2. 📊 Data Upload & Setup - Load data and configure cosmology parameters
3. 🌌 Module 1: Cosmic Evolution (Cos-Evo) - 21cm simulations
4. 🌟 Module 2: Cluster Environment Analyzer - Galaxy cluster analysis
5. 🔭 Module 3: JWST Spectrum Analyzer - NIRSpec spectroscopy pipeline
6. 📈 Comparative Dashboard - Integration across all modules
7. 📝 Report & Reflection - Generate scientific insights

Sidebar also contains Environment Status with optional dependency detection.

faqs.question: Where do I upload my data in the interface?
faqs.answer:
Data Upload Location:
Navigate to 📊 Data Upload & Setup tab in the sidebar.

Upload Options:
1. Galaxy Catalog (CSV/FITS)
   - Required columns: RA, Dec, redshift, and photometric bands
   - Supports both CSV and FITS formats

2. JWST/NIRSpec File (FITS)
   - Upload Stage 2 or Stage 3 JWST pipeline products
   - Optional—demo data available

Alternatively, load example datasets:
• Load Cluster Sample (mock data)
• Load Demo JWST Data (synthetic spectrum)
• Set Default Cosmology

All data is cached in st.session_state for performance.

faqs.question: How do I set cosmological parameters in Astro-AI?
faqs.answer:
Setting Cosmological Parameters:

1. Navigate to 📊 Data Upload & Setup → Cosmological Parameters tab

2. Adjust parameters using sliders/input boxes:
   • H₀ (km/s/Mpc): Typical range 50–100 (default: 67.66)
   • Ωₘ (matter density): 0.1–0.9 (default: 0.31)
   • σ₈ (matter amplitude): 0.6–1.2 (default: 0.8)
   • Redshift Range: 0–20 Gyr (default: 5–15)

3. Click "💾 Save Cosmology" to update session state.

4. Preset Option: Click "Default Cosmology" to restore Planck 2018 values.

These parameters drive all 21cm simulations and cosmic evolution calculations.

faqs.question: How do I run a 21cm cosmic evolution simulation?
faqs.answer:
Running 21cm Simulation:

1. Navigate to 🌌 Module 1: Cosmic Evolution (Cos-Evo)

2. Set simulation parameters:
   • Box Size (Mpc): 50, 100, or 200
   • Resolution (HII_DIM): 50, 100, or 128
   • Start Redshift: typically 15–20
   • End Redshift: typically 5–6
   • Redshift Step: typically 0.5–1.0

3. Click 🚀 Run 21cm Simulation
   - Results are cached automatically
   - Processing status shown with spinner

4. Results displayed in three tabs:
   • Global Signal: δTb vs redshift
   • Power Spectra: Δ²(k) evolution at each redshift
   • BT Slices: 2D brightness temperature at key redshifts

Note: Uses py21cmfast if available, otherwise falls back to physically motivated analytic model.

faqs.question: What do the 21cm simulation outputs mean?
faqs.answer:
Understanding 21cm Outputs:

1. Global Signal (δTb vs z):
   • Measures sky-averaged 21cm brightness temperature
   • Absorption trough near z~17–20 (Cosmic Dawn)
   • Signal rises through z~6–15 (reionization)
   • δTb is negative (absorption) or positive (emission)
   • Units: milliKelvin (mK)

2. Power Spectra (Δ²(k)):
   • Dimensionless power: Δ² = k³P(k)/(2π²)
   • Peak near k~0.1 Mpc⁻¹
   • Amplitude sensitive to ionization fraction and spin temperature
   • Colored lines represent different redshifts

3. Brightness Temperature Slices:
   • 2D maps of δTb at key redshifts
   • Show spatial structure of reionization
   • Color scale: red (hot/positive) to blue (cold/negative)

References: Pritchard & Loeb (2012), Furlanetto et al. (2006), Cohen et al. (2017).

faqs.question: What's the difference between mock and real 21cm simulations?
faqs.answer:
Mock vs Real 21cm Data:

REAL Simulations (py21cmfast installed):
• Uses full hydrodynamic 3D simulations
• Accurate reionization physics
• Computationally expensive
• Takes minutes to hours
• Label: None (unmarked)

MOCK Simulations (py21cmfast unavailable):
• Uses physically motivated analytic model
• Parameterized from Pritchard & Loeb (2012) and Furlanetto et al. (2006)
• Instant computation
• All mock results clearly marked "SIMULATED DATA" in yellow box
• Still scientifically valid for testing and learning

Key equation (analytic model):
δTb ≈ 27 x_HI (1 - T_CMB/T_S) × cosmological_factors [mK]

To run real simulations:
1. Install py21cmfast: pip install py21cmfast
2. Restart Streamlit: streamlit run app.py
3. App auto-detects py21cmfast and switches modes.

faqs.question: How do I run cluster analysis on galaxy data?
faqs.answer:
Running Cluster Analysis:

1. Load cluster data:
   • Upload CSV/FITS with columns: RA, Dec, redshift, stellar_mass, g_mag, r_mag
   • OR click "Load Cluster Sample" for mock data (recommended for testing)

2. Navigate to 🌟 Module 2: Cluster Environment Analyzer

3. Three analysis tabs:
   Tab 1 - Cluster Detection:
   • Plot RA-Dec spatial distribution
   • Detect clusters in redshift using gaussian_filter significance
   • Significance threshold: default 3.0σ

   Tab 2 - SED Fitting:
   • Configure Bagpipes model (SFH, dust, metallicity)
   • Run SED fitting with MultiNest (default n_live=400)
   • Results include stellar mass, age, SFR, metallicity, dust Av

   Tab 3 - Results:
   • Red fraction vs stellar mass
   • Environmental quenching efficiency
   • Color-magnitude diagrams
   • Stellar mass functions (SMF)

4. Environmental effects quantified via Peng+2010 model:
   • Additional red fraction in clusters: ~0.15 at fixed M*
   • Quenching efficiency = (f_red_cluster - f_red_field) / (1 - f_red_field)

NOTE: Mock data grounded in Baldry+2012 (field SMF) and Vulcani+2013 (cluster SMF).

faqs.question: What is the red fraction and why does it matter?
faqs.answer:
Red Fraction Explained:

Definition:
• f_red = fraction of red galaxies out of total galaxies
• Red galaxies identified by g–r color > 0.65 (Bell+2004)
• Already quenched (low star formation rate)

Why It Matters:
• Key indicator of galaxy quenching in different environments
• Mass dependence: more massive galaxies more likely red
• Environmental dependence: cluster galaxies more red than field
• Test of galaxy evolution models

Physical Interpretation:
• Environmental quenching in clusters suppresses star formation via:
  - Ram-pressure stripping of gas
  - Strangulation (prevention of gas accretion)
  - Tidal interactions
  - Harassment by cluster members

Peng+2010 Model:
f_red = 1 - (1 - ε_mass) × (1 - ε_env)
• ε_mass: mass-quenching efficiency (logistic at M* ~ 10.5 M☉)
• ε_env: environmental quenching (~0.15 in clusters)

Dashboard plots show red fraction vs stellar mass for cluster vs field galaxies.

faqs.question: What is the stellar mass function (SMF)?
faqs.answer:
Stellar Mass Function (SMF) Explained:

Definition:
φ(M*) = number density of galaxies per unit volume per unit mass [Mpc⁻³ dex⁻¹]
• Describes how many galaxies of each stellar mass exist
• Key constraint on galaxy formation models

Double-Schechter Parameterization (Baldry+2012):
φ(M*) = φ₁ × 10^[(1+α₁)(M-M*)]/2.5 × exp(-10^(M-M*)) + φ₂ × ... [two components]
• Faint-end slope α₁ ≈ –0.35 (field)
• Bright-end slope α₂ ≈ –1.47 (field)
• Characteristic mass log(M*) ≈ 10.66 M☉

Cluster vs Field Differences (Vulcani+2013):
• Cluster SMF shifted by +0.15 dex (more massive galaxies)
• Steeper faint-end slope (fewer dwarf galaxies)
• Due to environmental effects + starvation

In Astro-AI:
• Module 2 plots observed vs theoretical SMF
• Red vs blue SMF separately
• Mock data honor these published relations

References: Baldry et al. 2012, Vulcani et al. 2013.

faqs.question: How does SED fitting work in Astro-AI?
faqs.answer:
SED Fitting Pipeline (Bagpipes):

What is SED?
• Spectral Energy Distribution = flux vs wavelength
• Optical + NIR photometry provides multi-band flux points
• Input to SED fitting to constrain galaxy properties

Bagpipes Bayesian SED Fitting:
• Uses MultiNest nested sampling for posterior inference
• Flexible star formation history (SFH): exponential or double power-law
• Includes nebular emission, dust attenuation, ISM parameters
• Outputs posterior samples for stellar mass, age, SFR, metallicity, dust

Key Parameters Fitted:
• Redshift z (optionally)
• Stellar mass log(M*/M☉) [M☉]
• Age (Gyr)
• Tau / metallicity (Z/Z☉)
• Dust attenuation Av [mag], via Calzetti+2000 law
• Star formation rate (SFR) [M☉/yr]

Configuration in UI (Module 2):
• SFH Model: exponential (default) or double_power_law
• Dust Curve: Calzetti (default), SMC, MW
• Fit redshift? Option to fix or vary
• Mass/age/metallicity priors customizable via sliders

Accuracy:
• Stellar mass recovery: ±0.15 dex (photometric accuracy)
• Redshift: σ(z) ≈ 0.01 when spectroscopy available

Reference: Carnall et al. 2018 (Bagpipes), MNRAS 480, 4379.

faqs.question: How do I analyze JWST NIRSpec data in Astro-AI?
faqs.answer:
JWST Spectroscopy Analysis:

1. Load JWST Data:
   • Navigate to 📊 Data Upload & Setup
   • Upload FITS file (Stage 2 or Stage 3 product)
   • OR click "Load Demo JWST Data" for synthetic spectrum

2. Navigate to 🔭 Module 3: JWST Spectrum Analyzer

3. Process data through three pipeline stages:
   
   Stage 1 (Detector Processing):
   • Detector corrections: saturation, superbias, refpix, linearity, dark current, jump detection
   • Ramp fitting for non-destructive read sequences
   
   Stage 2 (Spectroscopic Processing):
   • WCS assignment (wavelength calibration)
   • MSA flagging
   • 2D spectral extraction
   • Flat-fielding
   • Pathloss correction
   • Photometric calibration
   
   Stage 3 (1D Extraction):
   • Optimal 1D extraction (Horne 1986 algorithm)
   • Removes spatial noise while preserving spectral features
   • Wavelength registration and resampling

4. Spectral Analysis:
   • 2D spectrum visualization
   • Extracted 1D spectrum with error bars
   • Emission line identification (Hα, Hβ, [OIII], etc.)
   • Spectral fitting with Bagpipes

5. Emission Lines Detected:
   • Rest-frame wavelengths defined in jWST_pipeline.py
   • Redshifted to observed frame
   • NIRSpec wavelength range: 0.6–5.3 μm at R~1000
   • Physical ratios from Case B recombination (Osterbrock & Ferland 2006)

Note: Mock data available when JWST pipeline not installed for testing.

faqs.question: What emission lines are typically found in JWST spectra, and what do they tell us?
faqs.answer:
Common JWST Emission Lines (Rest-frame Wavelengths):

Hydrogen Balmer Series:
• Hβ: 0.4861 μm — Baseline for line ratios
• Hα: 0.6563 μm — Star formation tracer, ~2.86× stronger than Hβ
• Hγ: 0.4341 μm, Hδ: 0.4102 μm — Weaker components

Forbidden [OIII] (Oxygen):
• [OIII]5007 Å (0.5007 μm) — Ionization indicator, scales with metallicity
• [OIII]4959 Å (0.4959 μm) — Fixed ratio ~0.34 to 5007 (atomic physics)
• High [OIII]/Hβ in metal-rich systems

Forbidden [NII] (Nitrogen):
• [NII]6548 Å, 6583 Å — Part of LINER diagnostics
• [NII]/Hα ratio sensitive to AGN activity

Paschen Series (NIR):
• Paβ: 1.2822 μm, Paα: 1.8751 μm — Visible in high-z spec

Physical Insights from Line Ratios:
• Balmer decrement Hα/Hβ: dust attenuation (Calzetti+2000 law)
• [OIII]/Hβ: metallicity via O3N2 calibration (Pettini & Pagel 2004)
• BPT Diagram ([OIII]/Hβ vs [NII]/Hα): star-forming vs AGN vs composite
• Equivalent widths: age and SFR indicators

In Astro-AI:
• Lines automatically identified in 1D spectrum
• Wavelengths corrected for redshift
• Bagpipes fits line fluxes to constrain SFR and metallicity

References: Osterbrock & Ferland (2006), Pettini & Pagel (2004), Kewley & Dopita (2002).

faqs.question: How is dust attenuation handled in JWST analysis?
faqs.answer:
Dust Attenuation in Astro-AI:

Calzetti+2000 Dust Law:
• Parameterised attenuation curve k(λ) [mag/mag]
• Applies to both stellar continuum and emission lines
• Covers UV to NIR (0.12–2.2 μm rest-frame)

Attenuation Formula:
Flux_obs = Flux_intrinsic × 10^(-0.4 × Av × k(λ) / R_V)
• Av = V-band attenuation [mag]
• R_V = 4.05 (Calzetti+2000, starburst galaxies)
• k(λ) = wavelength-dependent curve

Line-specific Attenuation:
• Lines suffer extra dust: E(B-V)_gas = Av / (R_V × 0.44)
• Stars see less dust: E(B-V)_stars = 0.44 × E(B-V)_gas
• Effect: nebular lines fainter than stellar continuum at same intrinsic level

Empirical Constraints:
• Balmer decrement Hα/Hβ intrinsic = 2.86 (Case B, T=10⁴K)
• Observed ratio = 2.86 × 10^(-0.4 × Av × [k(Hα) - k(Hβ)])
• Measure observed ratio → solve for Av

Bagpipes Fitting:
• Av is a free parameter fit to observed photometry/spectrum
• Config in Module 2/3: Dust Curve (Calzetti, SMC, MW)
• Typical range: Av = 0–2 mag

Interpretation:
• Av ~ 0 mag: dust-free (rare in real galaxies)
• Av ~ 0.5 mag: modest dust (typical star-forming z~2)
• Av ~ 1–2 mag: heavily obscured starburst
• Av > 2 mag: very dusty, ultraluminous infrared galaxies (ULIRGs)

Reference: Calzetti et al. 2000, ApJ 533, 682.

faqs.question: How do I configure Bagpipes SED fitting parameters?
faqs.answer:
Bagpipes Configuration in Astro-AI:

1. Star Formation History (SFH) Model:
   Option A - Exponential (Recommended):
   • Age: 0.1–15 Gyr (when did star formation start?)
   • Tau (τ): 0.3–10 Gyr (timescale of exponential decay)
   • Formula: SFR(t) ∝ e^(-t/τ)
   • Best for passive + intermediate galaxies

   Option B - Double Power-Law:
   • Alpha (α), Beta (β): steepness of rise and fall
   • More flexible for complex SFH
   • Better for starbursts and irregulars

2. Stellar Population Priors:
   • Mass Formed (M_formed): 1–15 dex [log M☉]
   • Metallicity (Z/Z☉): 0–2.5 [solar units]

3. Dust Model:
   • Curve: Calzetti (default - starburst), SMC, or Milky Way
   • Av Range: 0–3 mag (V-band attenuation)
   • Younger starbursts: higher Av
   • Old ellipticals: Av ≈ 0

4. Redshift:
   • Option 1: Fit redshift (if spectrum available)
   • Option 2: Fix redshift (if photometry only)
   • Range: 0–10 (z > 10 rare)

5. Nested Sampling (MultiNest):
   • n_live: Default 400
   • Higher n_live: more accurate but slower (n_live=800 recommended for publication)
   • Lower n_live: faster but less precise

UI Workflow (Module 2 - SED Fitting Tab):
1. Select SFH model
2. Set priors with sliders
3. Set dust curve and Av range
4. Choose fit redshift option
5. Click "🔄 Run SED Fitting"
6. Results in "Results" tab:
   • stellar_mass ± error [M☉]
   • sfr ± error [M☉/yr]
   • age ± error [Gyr]
   • metallicity ± error [Z/Z☉]
   • dust Av ± error [mag]

Reference: Carnall et al. 2018 (Bagpipes), MNRAS 480, 4379.

faqs.question: How do I interpret Bagpipes SED fitting results?
faqs.answer:
Interpreting SED Fitting Outputs:

Key Parameters:

1. Stellar Mass (log M*/M☉):
   • Precision: ±0.15 dex typical (photometric accuracy)
   • 10^10 M☉ = massive elliptical
   • 10^9 M☉ = large spiral
   • 10^8 M☉ = dwarf galaxy
   • Compare derived mass vs prior range to check fit quality

2. Age (Gyr):
   • Young: < 1 Gyr (starburst, active star formation)
   • Intermediate: 1–5 Gyr (post-starburst, passive)
   • Old: > 5 Gyr (elliptical, quenched)
   • Error bar indicates posterior uncertainty
   • If Error ~ Age, posterior multi-modal (poor constraints)

3. SFR (M☉/yr):
   • High SFR: > 10 M☉/yr (normal star-forming)
   • Moderate: 1–10 M☉/yr (declining SFR)
   • Low: < 1 M☉/yr (quenched, old)
   • Must measure from Hα, [OIII], or UV—SED gives SFR history average

4. Metallicity (Z/Z☉):
   • Solar: Z/Z☉ ≈ 1.0
   • Metal-poor: Z/Z☉ < 0.3 (dwarf galaxies, early universe)
   • Metal-rich: Z/Z☉ > 1.0 (massive galaxies, bulges)
   • Constrained mainly by UV continuum slope + optical line ratios

5. Dust Av (mag):
   • Av ≈ 0: dust-free or nearly so (rare)
   • Av ~ 0.3–0.7: typical star-forming galaxies
   • Av > 1: dusty starbursts
   • Check via Balmer decrement if emission lines observed

6. Posterior Samples:
   • Plot 2D distributions (masses vs age, age vs sfr, etc.)
   • Covariances revealed (e.g., age–dust degeneracy)
   • Confidence intervals from percentiles (e.g., 16th–84th)

Quality Checks:
• Compare model spectrum overlay with observed photometry
• Look for chi²_min / N_bands (should be ~1 per band)
• Residuals >3σ indicate bad bands or model mismatch
• Re-run with different SFH if results seem degenerate

ReferenceFrame: Carnall et al. 2018, ApJ 873, 44.

faqs.question: SED fitting diverged or gave unexpected results. What should I do?
faqs.answer:
Troubleshooting SED Fitting:

Common Issues:

1. Divergence or non-convergence:
   • Cause: Priors too narrow or no data
   • Fix:
     - Relax priors (widen age/mass ranges in sliders)
     - Increase n_live (e.g., 800 instead of 400)
     - Check data: are photometric errors realistic?

2. Age–dust degeneracy (age error >> posterior):
   • Young model with dust can mimic old model with no dust
   • Common when only optical data available
   • Fix: Include UV or NIR spectroscopy
   • Include a prior or informative likelihood

3. Unrealistic metallicity (Z/Z☉ outside 0–2 range):
   • Usually means insufficient spectroscopy
   • Fix: Tighten metallicity prior (0.3–2.0 range)
   • Add emission line diagnostics if available

4. SFR = 0 but age error huge:
   • Model has freedom in SFH—no strong constraint
   • Fix: Include Hα or infrared luminosity measurement

5. Error bars too large (unrealistic posterior):
   • Photometric errors probably too large
   • Fix: Review input catalogs for outliers or bad measurements
   • Mask suspect bands

Advanced Diagnostics:
• Plot model spectrum vs observed photometry
• Check for systematic residuals (e.g., all points above model)
• Inspect corner plots (posterior samples 2D projections)
• Run parameter recovery tests (inject mock data, re-fit)

Re-running Fit:
• Change priors in UI → 🔄 Run SED Fitting
• Cached results will update automatically
• If still diverged, click "Clear Cache" (clears st.session_state)

Reference: Carnall et al. 2018, MNRAS 480, 4379; Tips in INSTALL.md.

faqs.question: How do I get AI-powered scientific insights on my analysis results?
faqs.answer:
AI-Powered Insights in Astro-AI:

Feature Overview:
• AI engine powered by OpenAI/OpenRouter (DeepSeek R1 free tier by default)
• Generates scientific interpretations of simulation/analysis results
• Integrates with RAG (Retrieval-Augmented Generation) for context

1. Setup (One-time):
   • Get API key:
     - OpenAI: https://platform.openai.com/api/keys
     - OpenRouter: https://openrouter.ai/keys
   • Add to .streamlit/secrets.toml:
     OPENROUTER_API_KEY = "sk-or-v1-..."
     OPENROUTER_MODEL = "deepseek/deepseek-r1:free"
   • Restart Streamlit: streamlit run app.py

2. Usage (In UI):
   • Run any analysis (Cosmic Evolution, Cluster, JWST)
   • Navigate to 📝 Report & Reflection
   • Select template or write custom query
   • AI generates ~1500 token response in 10–30 seconds

3. Available Templates:
   • "Cosmic evolution timeline analysis"
   • "Environmental effects on galaxy properties"
   • "Spectroscopic vs photometric constraints"
   • "High-redshift galaxy formation insights"
   • Custom queries

4. Result Examples:
   • Cosmic Evolution:
     "Your 21cm signal shows absorption minimum at z=18.5, suggesting cosmic dawn at this epoch. Reionization midpoint appears at z=8.2, consistent with Planck 2020 constraints..."
   • Cluster Analysis:
     "Red fraction enhancement in clusters (Δf_red ≈ 0.12) implies environmental quenching efficiency ~40% at M*=10.5 M☉, lower than local universe (Peng+2010 z~0.05). Possible evolution or redshift..."

5. RAG Context:
   • AI retrieves relevant scientific knowledge (top 3 documents)
   • Combines retrieved context + your analysis results
   • Improves accuracy by grounding in astrophysical background

Common Issues:
• "Rate limit (HTTP 429)" → Wait 5 min and retry
• "No API key" → Set OPENROUTER_API_KEY in secrets.toml
• Nonsensical response → Check RAG knowledge base or simplify query

Note: All queries return fallback message if real API unavailable (graceful degradation).

faqs.question: How do I set up OpenRouter with Astro-AI?
faqs.answer:
OpenRouter Setup Guide:

1. Create OpenRouter Account:
   • Go to https://openrouter.ai
   • Sign up (free tier available)
   • Accept terms

2. Get API Key:
   • Login to dashboard
   • Navigate to "Keys" or "API"
   • Generate new key: click "Create new key"
   • Copy the key (starts with "sk-or-v1-...")
   • KEEP IT SECRET — treat like password

3. Set API Key in Astro-AI:
   • Create/edit .streamlit/secrets.toml:
     [secrets]
     OPENROUTER_API_KEY = "sk-or-v1-YOUR_KEY_HERE"
     OPENROUTER_MODEL = "deepseek/deepseek-r1:free"

   • On Streamlit Cloud, add via Secrets management:
     • App menu → ⚙️ Settings → Secrets
     • Add same key/value pairs

4. Restart Streamlit:
   • Local: streamlit run app.py
   • Cloud: Auto-restarts on secrets change

5. Verify Setup:
   • Launch app
   • Check sidebar Environment Status:
     • ✅ OpenRouter connected?
     • Check AI status in Report & Reflection

Alternative Models (if you prefer):
• "deepseek/deepseek-r1:free" (default, free, good for reasoning)
• "anthropic/claude-3.5-sonnet" (paid, faster)
• "openai/gpt-4o" (paid, most capable)
• Full list: https://openrouter.ai/api/v1/models

Costs (OpenRouter):
• DeepSeek R1 free: $0/token (free tier)
• Claude 3.5 Sonnet: ~$0.003 per 1K input tokens
• GPT-4o: ~$0.005 per 1K input tokens
• Usage typically 800–1500 tokens per analysis

Free Tier Limits:
• Requests per minute: ~10
• Total requests: unlimited (free DeepSeek)
• Careful with batch operations

Troubleshooting:
• "Invalid API key" → Check copy/paste, no spaces
• "Unauthorized" → Key may be expired or deleted
• "Rate limited" → Wait 5 minutes, reduce batch size

Reference: https://openrouter.ai/docs

faqs.question: What is RAG and how does the knowledge base work?
faqs.answer:
RAG System Explained (Retrieval-Augmented Generation):

What is RAG?
• Hybrid AI system: retrieval + generation
• Retrieves relevant documents from knowledge base
• Feeds context to LLM for informed responses
• Much more accurate than LLM alone

Astro-AI RAG Architecture:

1. Knowledge Base:
   • TF-IDF vectorization of scientific content
   • ~30 astrophysical explanations (default)
   • User analysis results automatically stored
   • File: data/astro_knowledge_base.json + vectors.pkl

2. Retrieval Pipeline:
   • User query → tokenize + lowercase + remove stopwords
   • Compute TF-IDF vector from query
   • Cosine similarity vs all documents
   • Retrieve top 3 most relevant documents (default top_k=3)
   • Score typically 0.0–1.0 (0.4 is meaningful match)

3. Context Generation:
   • Format retrieved docs as "RETRIEVED CONTEXT" block
   • Include relevance score and metadata (type, source module)
   • Pass to AI along with user query

4. Why It Works:
   • AI grounded in actual knowledge (reduces hallucinations)
   • Answers supported by examples
   • Can cite sources ("From cosmic_evolution module...")
   • Consistent with documented physics

Default Knowledge Base Content:
• 21cm intensity mapping and reionization (Pritchard & Loeb)
• Galaxy clusters and quenching (Peng+2010)
• JWST NIRSpec capabilities
• Bagpipes SED fitting methodology
• Stellar mass functions (Baldry+2012)
• Emission line diagnostics
• NIRSpec 21cm power spectrum

Automatic Knowledge Addition:
• Each scientific analysis stored with metadata
• Type: "scientific_result"
• Source: module name (cos_evo, cluster_analyzer, jwst_analyzer)
• Results automatically retrievable in future AI queries

Example Flow:
User Query: "Why is my cluster red fraction so high?"
→ RAG retrieves:
  [1] Document on red fraction definition & Peng+2010 model (score=0.68)
  [2] Document on environmental quenching (score=0.55)
  [3] Document on stellar mass functions (score=0.42)
→ AI generates answer combining retrieved context + user results
→ Response mentions Peng+2010 by name and explains physics

Check RAG Status:
• Sidebar → Environment Status → Expander
• Shows: enabled/disabled, document count, vocabulary size
• API: call get_rag_status() or query_knowledge_base()

Reference: rag_system.py, AstroRAGSystem class.

faqs.question: I see a 'Module not found' or import error. How do I fix it?
faqs.answer:
Troubleshooting Import Errors:

Common Import Errors:

1. ModuleNotFoundError: No module named 'astropy'
   Cause: Astropy not installed
   Fix:
   • pip install astropy
   • Or: pip install -r requirements.txt
   • Restart Streamlit: streamlit run app.py

2. ModuleNotFoundError: No module named 'py21cmfast'
   Cause: Optional dependency not installed
   Fix:
   • This is optional—app falls back to analytic model
   • To install: pip install py21cmfast (slow, requires fortran compiler)
   • Or use mock mode (default)

3. ModuleNotFoundError: No module named 'bagpipes'
   Cause: Bagpipes not installed
   Fix:
   • pip install bagpipes
   • Bagpipes requires BAGPIPES_DATA environment variable
   • App attempts auto-setup; see ID #4 for manual setup

4. ImportError: No module named 'jwst_pipeline'
   Cause: JWST pipeline not installed (optional)
   Fix:
   • This is optional—app uses mock JWST data
   • To install: pip install jwst (large download, ~500MB)
   • Requires astropy, numpy, scipy, h5py

5. Error setting BAGPIPES environment variables (app.py startup):
   Cause: Permission issues or invalid temp directory
   Fix:
   • Check temp directory exists: /tmp (Linux/macOS) or C:\Users\*\AppData\Local\Temp (Windows)
   • Or set manually: export BAGPIPES_DATA=/your/path
   • Then restart app

Detect Missing Dependencies:
• Sidebar → Environment Status → Check✅ boxes
• Missing packages shown in yellow
• Strict mode available: checkbox in Environment Status expander
• When strict=True, app fails if library marked required is missing

Full Diagnostic:
• Run: python -c "import module_name"
• Gives clear error message
• Example: python -c "import py21cmfast" → ImportError if missing

Install All Dependencies:
• requirements.txt: pip install -r requirements.txt (core + common)
• requirements-full.txt: everything including py21cmfast (may fail on some systems)
• requirements-minimal.txt: barebone Streamlit + numpy only

Clean Install:
• Remove old environment: rm -rf venv/
• Create fresh: python -m venv venv
• Activate: source venv/bin/activate (Linux/macOS) or venv\Scripts\activate (Windows)
• Install: pip install --upgrade pip setuptools wheel
• Then: pip install -r requirements.txt

Streamlit Cloud Deployment:
• Ensure dependencies in requirements.txt
• System packages in packages.txt (apt-get installable)
• Example packages.txt:
   libffi-dev
   gfortran
   libopenblas-dev

Reference: INSTALL.md, feature_flags.py.

faqs.question: The app crashed or is showing a red error box. What does it mean?
faqs.answer:
Understanding and Fixing Streamlit Errors:

Streamlit Error Display:
• Red box with traceback = script exception
• Yellow box with warning = non-fatal (app continues)
• Orange box = deprecated feature

Common Crashes:

1. "ValueError: No data loaded"
   Cause: Clicked Run Simulation before loading data
   Fix: Load data first (Data Upload tab → Load Cluster Sample)
   Code: Always check `if self.data is None` before analysis

2. "TypeError: unsupported operand type(s)"
   Cause: Type mismatch (e.g., adding string + number)
   Fix: Check data types in your uploaded CSV
   Example: redshift column should be float, not string
   Solution: pd.to_numeric(df['redshift'], errors='coerce')

3. "MemoryError"
   Cause: Not enough RAM for simulation
   Fix: Reduce box_size or resolution parameters
   Example: use 50 Mpc / 50 HII_DIM instead of 200 Mpc / 128

4. "RecursionError: maximum recursion depth exceeded"
   Cause: Circular dependency or infinite loop in analysis
   Fix: Restart Streamlit: streamlit run app.py --logger.level=debug
   Check for circular function calls

5. "NameError: name 'X' is not defined"
   Cause: Variable not initialized or typo
   Fix: Check session state initialization in _init_session_state()
   In app.py line ~120:
   defaults = {"my_var": value}  # ensure your var is here

6. "AttributeError: 'DataFrame' has no attribute 'X'"
   Cause: Column name mismatch
   Fix: Print available columns: print(df.columns)
   Update column_mapping in ClusterAnalyzer.__init__()

7. "FileNotFoundError: 'data/file.csv'"
   Cause: Uploaded file path not found
   Fix: Check file uploaded successfully (session_state['uploaded_data'])
   Debug: print(st.session_state.uploaded_data)

Debug Mode:
• Enable debug logging:
   streamlit run app.py --logger.level=debug
• Prints detailed stack traces
• Outputs go to terminal, not web UI

Clear Cached State:
• Click browser refresh (⟲) button
• Or restart Streamlit: Ctrl+C then streamlit run app.py
• Clears all session_state variables
• Useful after major code changes

Check Logs (Streamlit Cloud):
• App menu → ⓘ Manage app
• View Logs section
• Search for ERROR or WARNING

Common Fixes (in order):
1. Restart app
2. Clear browser cache (Ctrl+Shift+Delete)
3. Check Python version: python --version (must be 3.10+)
4. Reinstall dependencies: pip install --upgrade -r requirements.txt
5. Create fresh venv (see ID #25 for clean install)

Report Issues:
• Include full error traceback
• Describe steps to reproduce
• Share your input data (if possible)
• Contact: redwanrahman2002@outlook.com or camsust@gmail.com

Reference: app.py exception handling, logging configuration.

faqs.question: The app is running very slowly. How can I speed it up?
faqs.answer:
Performance Troubleshooting:

Identify Bottleneck:
• Streamlit profiler: streamlit run app.py --logger.level=debug --client.showErrorDetails=True
• Browser DevTools (F12 → Network tab) to measure load times
• Check terminal for slow operations (look for timestamps)

Optimization Strategies:

1. Use Caching (@st.cache_data and @st.cache_resource):
   ✅ Already implemented in app.py for:
   • Simulation results (1-hour TTL, hash_funcs not needed)
   • Mock cluster data (30-min TTL)
   • Analyzer instances (singleton, reused)
   
   ❌ If you add new heavy functions:
   @st.cache_data(ttl=3600)  # cache for 1 hour
   def expensive_analysis(params):
       return results

2. Reduce Simulation Parameters:
   • Box Size: 50 Mpc instead of 200 (8× faster, 512³ vs 128000³ voxels)
   • Resolution: 50 instead of 128 (17× faster)
   • Redshift Steps: use 1.0 or 2.0 instead of 0.5 (2× faster)

3. Lazy Figure Generation:
   ✅ Already done: figures cached, only regenerated on parameter change
   ❌ If you're creating plots on every rerun:
   Use @st.cache_data to store figures

4. Reduce Data Size:
   • Galaxy catalog: keep N < 10,000 for real-time cluster analysis
   • JPEG compression for images before upload
   • Downsample spectra if not needed at full resolution

5. Vectorize Operations:
   ❌ Bad (slow):
   for i in range(len(df)): result += df.iloc[i]['column']
   
   ✅ Good (fast):
   result = df['column'].sum()
   # Use NumPy/Pandas vectorized operations

6. Profile Memory Usage:
   • Check RAM: ps aux | grep streamlit (Linux) or Task Manager (Windows)
   • If > 2GB: reduce dataset size or split into pages
   • Use memory_profiler: pip install memory-profiler
   
7. Offload to Background:
   • Use @st.cache_resource for one-time initialization
   • Avoid re-initializing models on every page load
   ✅ Cluster/JWST/CosmicEvolution instances cached

8. Pagination:
   • For large result tables: show top 100 rows, paginate rest
   • Example: df[i*100:(i+1)*100] with "Next Page" button

9. Simplify UX:
   • Remove unnecessary re-renders (check st.session_state conditions)
   • Use st.selectbox() instead of st.radio() for many options
   • Defer heavy plots to expander (only render when opened)

10. Server Resources (Streamlit Cloud):
    • Free tier: 1 CPU, 512 MB RAM
    • Upgrade to paid tier for better performance
    • Or deploy locally on better hardware

Monitoring:
• Check Network tab (DevTools) for POST times
• Typical response: 1–3 sec for simulation
• > 10 sec indicates issue

Py21cmfast Speedup:
• Use smaller grids (HII_DIM=50, BOX_LEN=50)
• GPUs can accelerate (not enabled by default)
• For research: run offline, save results, load in Streamlit

Reference: https://docs.streamlit.io/develop/concepts/architecture/caching

faqs.question: My data is in format X. How do I import it into Astro-AI?
faqs.answer:
Data Format Support:

Supported Formats:

1. CSV (Comma-Separated Values):
   ✅ Upload directly via UI (Data Upload tab)
   ✅ Required columns: RA, Dec, redshift, stellar_mass (or log10_stellar_mass)
   ✅ Optional columns: g_mag, r_mag, cl_id, halo_mass
   
   Example CSV:
   RA,Dec,redshift,stellar_mass,g_mag,r_mag,cl_id
   150.1,2.0,1.2,1e10,23.5,22.8,cluster_1
   150.15,2.01,1.21,5e9,24.2,23.1,cluster_1
   ...
   
   Python load:
   from utils.data_handler import DataHandler
   dh = DataHandler()
   df = dh.load_csv('my_catalog.csv')

2. FITS (Flexible Image Transport System):
   ✅ Upload directly via UI (JWST data)
   ✅ Supported FITS types:
   • Multi-HDU FITS with SCI/ERR/DQ extensions (JWST pipeline output)
   • FITS table (astropy.table.Table)
   
   When loaded:
   • SCI → flux data
   • ERR → noise/error array
   • DQ → data quality flags
   
   Python load:
   from astropy.io import fits
   with fits.open('data.fits') as hdul:
       sci_data = hdul['SCI'].data
       err_data = hdul['ERR'].data

3. HDF5 (Hierarchical Data Format):
   ✅ Python-only (install h5py: pip install h5py)
   ✅ Call via DataHandler.load_hdf5(filename)
   ✅ Stores datasets as arrays + attributes
   
   Python usage:
   from utils.data_handler import DataHandler
   dh = DataHandler(data_dir='data')
   data = dh.load_hdf5('results.h5')
   # data['key'].shape, data['attr'], etc.

4. Pickle (Python object serialization):
   ✅ Python-only (fast for testing)
   ✅ Load via DataHandler.load_pickle(filename)
   ✅ Contains Python dicts/lists/arrays
   
   Example:
   data = dh.load_pickle('cached_results.pkl')

5. JSON (JavaScript Object Notation):
   ✅ Web format, human-readable
   ✅ Load via DataHandler.load_json(filename)  - NOT YET IMPLEMENTED
   ✅ Use Python: import json; data = json.load(open(file))

Data Conversion Tools:

ASCII → CSV:
• Use Excel or text editor to convert to comma-separated format
• Or Python:
  import pandas as pd
  df = pd.read_csv('data.txt', delim_whitespace=True)
  df.to_csv('data.csv', index=False)

FITS → CSV:
  from astropy.io import fits
  from astropy.table import Table
  table = Table.read('data.fits')
  table.write('data.csv', format='ascii.commented_header', overwrite=True)

Database (SQLite) → CSV:
  import sqlite3
  conn = sqlite3.connect('database.db')
  df = pd.read_sql('SELECT * FROM table_name', conn)
  df.to_csv('data.csv', index=False)

Column Name Requirements:

Cluster Analysis Expects:
• RA [degrees]: Right Ascension
• Dec [degrees]: Declination
• redshift: galaxy redshift z
• stellar_mass [M☉] OR log10_stellar_mass: M* (required)
• g_mag [mag]: g-band magnitude (optional, for color)
• r_mag [mag]: r-band magnitude (optional, for color)
• cl_id: cluster membership label (optional)
• halo_mass [M☉]: halo mass (optional)

JWST Analysis Expects:
• FITS HDU structure (SCI, ERR, DQ extensions)
• 2D array for Stage 2 data
• 1D wavelength array with WCS header

Tip: If column names don't match, update column_mapping in ClusterAnalyzer.__init__() or JWSTAnalyzer.

Validation:
• Streamlit UI auto-validates column presence
• Numeric columns must be numeric (not strings)
• Use pd.to_numeric(df['col'], errors='coerce') to convert
• NaN values tolerance depends on analysis (typically OK if < 10%)

Reference: utils/data_handler.py, DataHandler class.

faqs.question: AI features aren't working. What should I check?
faqs.answer:
AI Features Troubleshooting (OpenRouter/DeepSeek):

Check AI Status:
1. Sidebar → Environment Status → Expander
2. Look for OpenRouter/OpenAI API status indicator
3. Should show: "API connected" or "Simulation mode"

Common Issues:

1. "API key not found or invalid"
   Cause: OPENROUTER_API_KEY not set or mistyped
   Fix:
   a) Local development:
      • Create .streamlit/secrets.toml in project root
      • Add: OPENROUTER_API_KEY = "sk-or-v1-YOUR_ACTUAL_KEY"
      • Restart: streamlit run app.py
      • Verify: app tries to fetch from secrets
   
   b) Streamlit Cloud:
      • Click app menu (⋯) → Settings → Secrets
      • Paste same key/value
      • Changes take effect in ~30 seconds
   
   c) Verify key validity:
      • Login to https://openrouter.ai
      • Check your keys haven't expired or been revoked
      • If revoked, generate new key

2. "Rate limited (HTTP 429)"
   Cause: Too many API requests in short time
   Fix:
   • Wait 5 minutes before next query
   • Free tier: ~10 requests/minute limit
   • Reduce batch size if running multiple analyses

3. "Connection timeout"
   Cause: Network issue or OpenRouter servers down
   Fix:
   • Check internet connection
   • Try again in 1 minute
   • Check https://openrouter.ai/status
   • If down, app falls back to simulation mode

4. "No model specified"
   Cause: OPENROUTER_MODEL not set
   Fix:
   • Default (in code if not set): "deepseek/deepseek-r1:free"
   • Optional set in secrets.toml:
     OPENROUTER_MODEL = "deepseek/deepseek-r1:free"
   • Restart after change

5. "Nonsensical or blank response"
   Cause: RAG knowledge base empty or query too vague
   Fix:
   • RAG auto-populates with defaults on first run
   • Try more specific query (e.g., "What does my red fraction mean?")
   • Check RAG status: sidebar → Environment Status → look for document count

6. "404 Not Found" for model name
   Cause: Model name misspelled or deprecated
   Fix:
   • Check available models: https://openrouter.ai/api/v1/models
   • Correct spelling in secrets.toml
   • Restart app
   • Examples: "deepseek/deepseek-r1:free", "anthropic/claude-3.5-sonnet"

7. "Payment required" message
   Cause: Free tier credits exhausted or payment method needed
   Fix:
   • Free DeepSeek R1: no payment required, unlimited
   • Other models (Claude, GPT-4): require payment method on file
   • Switch to free model: OPENROUTER_MODEL = "deepseek/deepseek-r1:free"
   • Or add payment method to OpenRouter account

Debug Commands:

• Check API connection:
  python -c "import os; print(os.getenv('OPENROUTER_API_KEY'))"
  # Should print your key (masked in some contexts)

• Test RAG:
  python
  from utils.rag_system import get_rag_system
  rag = get_rag_system()
  print(rag.get_stats())
  # Should show document count > 0

• Test OpenRouter directly:
  import os
  from openai import OpenAI
  client = OpenAI(api_key=os.getenv('OPENROUTER_API_KEY'), 
                   base_url='https://openrouter.ai/api/v1')
  resp = client.chat.completions.create(
      model='deepseek/deepseek-r1:free',
      messages=[{'role': 'user', 'content': 'Hi'}]
  )
  print(resp.choices[0].message.content)
  # If works, integration is fine

Fallback Behavior:
• If API fails: "Simulation mode" activates
• Response generated from templates (not AI)
• Still usable but less personalized
• User sees warning: "AI features temporarily unavailable"

Report AI Issues:
• Include your OpenRouter account email (NOT your API key!)
• Share exact error message/trace
• Note whether other features work (helps isolate problem)
• Contact: redwanrahman2002@outlook.com

Reference: api/openai_integration.py, _configure_provider().

faqs.question: How do I export my analysis results?
faqs.answer:
Exporting Analysis Results:

Available Export Formats:

1. CSV (Comma-Separated Values):
   ✅ Best for: Table data, galaxy catalogs, detection results
   ✅ Easy to share and open in Excel / Google Sheets
   
   Export from module:
   from utils.data_handler import DataHandler
   dh = DataHandler(data_dir='output')
   dh.save_csv(results_df, 'my_results.csv')
   
   Via UI:
   • Run analysis → Results tab → (button to download CSV will be added)

2. JSON (JavaScript Object Notation):
   ✅ Best for: Nested results, metadata, API interchange
   ✅ Human-readable, preserves structure
   
   Python export:
   import json
   from utils.data_handler import _NumpyEncoder
   with open('results.json', 'w') as f:
       json.dump(results_dict, f, cls=_NumpyEncoder, indent=2)
   
   Note: _NumpyEncoder handles numpy types automatically

3. HDF5 (Hierarchical Data Format):
   ✅ Best for: Large 3D arrays (brightness temperature cubes)
   ✅ Keeps metadata alongside data
   ✅ Binary (compact), efficient for reading subsets
   
   Export:
   from utils.data_handler import DataHandler
   dh = DataHandler(data_dir='output')
   data_dict = {
       'brightness_temp': cube,        # 3D array
       'redshifts': z_array,            # 1D array
       'power_spectra': ps_list,        # list of arrays
   }
   dh.save_hdf5(data_dict, 'sim_results.h5')
   
   Read back:
   loaded_data = dh.load_hdf5('sim_results.h5')
   cube = loaded_data['brightness_temp']

4. FITS (for spectra):
   ✅ Best for: Astronomical spectra, images
   ✅ Standard in astrophysics
   
   Export 1D spectrum:
   from astropy.table import Table
   spectrum = Table({
       'wavelength': wavelength_array,
       'flux': flux_array,
       'flux_error': flux_error_array,
   })
   spectrum.write('spectrum_1d.fits', overwrite=True)

5. Pickle (Python binary):
   ✅ Best for: Caching intermediate results
   ✅ Only use for Python pipelines (not shareable)
   
   Export:
   dh.save_pickle(results_dict, 'cache.pkl')

6. Figure Images (PNG/PDF):
   ✅ Best for: Publication, presentations
   ✅ All plots automatically saveable
   
   Matplotlib (used in plots):
   fig, ax = plt.subplots()
   ax.plot(...)
   fig.savefig('plot.png', dpi=300, bbox_inches='tight')
   fig.savefig('plot.pdf')  # vector format, scalable
   
   Streamlit UI:
   • Hover over any plot → download icon (⬇️)
   • Auto-named by figure type

Step-by-Step Export Workflows:

Workflow 1: Export Full Cosmic Evolution Results
```python
from modules.cos_evo.cosmic_evolution import CosmicEvolution
from utils.data_handler import DataHandler, _NumpyEncoder
import json

cos_evo = CosmicEvolution({"H0": 67.66, "Om0": 0.31, "sigma8": 0.8})
results = cos_evo.run_simulation(box_size=100, resolution=100, ...)

# Save to HDF5 (for brightness temps)
dh = DataHandler()
dh.save_hdf5(
    {'bt_cubes': results['brightness_temperatures'],
     'redshifts': results['redshifts']},
    'cosmic_evo.h5'
)

# Save summary to JSON
with open('cosmic_evo_summary.json', 'w') as f:
    json.dump(
        {'redshifts': list(results['redshifts']),
         'global_signals': list(results['global_signals']),
         'sim_params': results['simulation_params']},
        f, cls=_NumpyEncoder, indent=2
    )
```

Workflow 2: Export Cluster Analysis Results
```python
from modules.cluster_analyzer.cluster_analysis import ClusterAnalyzer

ca = ClusterAnalyzer()
ca.load_data('catalog.csv')
ca.separate_cluster_field()
rf_results = ca.compute_red_fraction_vs_mass()

# Export
dh = DataHandler()
dh.save_csv(rf_results['cluster_members'], 'cluster_members.csv')
dh.save_csv(rf_results['field_galaxies'], 'field_galaxies.csv')

# Also save red fraction metrics
import json
with open('red_fraction_metrics.json', 'w') as f:
    json.dump(rf_results, f, cls=_NumpyEncoder, indent=2)
```

Workflow 3: Export JWST Spectral Fit Results
```python
from modules.jwst_analyzer.jwst_pipeline import JWSTAnalyzer

ja = JWSTAnalyzer()
ja.load_jwst_data('spectrum.fits')
ja.run_pipeline_stage1()
lja.run_pipeline_stage2()
lja.run_pipeline_stage3()

# Extract and save 1D spectrum
spec_1d = ja.extract_1d_spectrum()

# Save as FITS (preserves full header)
from astropy.table import Table
Table(spec_1d).write('spectrum_1d_extracted.fits', format='fits', overwrite=True)

# Save spectral fit results
fit_results = ja.run_spectral_fitting()
dh = DataHandler()
dh.save_json(fit_results, 'spectral_fits.json')
```

File Organization Best Practices:
```
output/
├── cosmic_evo/
│   ├── sim_results.h5           # binary, large
│   ├── summary.json             # metadata
│   └── plots/
│       ├── global_signal.png
│       └── power_spectra.pdf
├── cluster/
│   ├── cluster_members.csv
│   ├── field_galaxies.csv
│   └── analysis_summary.json
└── jwst/
    ├── spectrum_1d.fits
    └── spectral_fits.json
```

Sharing Results:
• CSV: email, cloud storage (Google Drive, Dropbox)
• JSON: web APIs, Slack, GitHub
• FITS: archival storage, astronomy databases
• HDF5: within research team (standard for large datasets)
• PNG: presentations, papers
• PDF: publication-ready

Reference: utils/data_handler.py, DataHandler class methods.

faqs.question: How do I check if my data is valid before uploading?
faqs.answer:
Data Validation Checklist:

Before Uploading:

1. File Format Check:
   ✅ CSV: Open in text editor or Excel → verify comma-separated
   ✅ FITS: Use Python:
      from astropy.io import fits
      fits.info('file.fits')  # Lists all HDUs and their structure
   ✅ Check file size:
      • CSV should be < 100 MB (typical upload limit)
      • FITS < 500 MB recommended

2. Column Names Validation:
   Required for Cluster Analysis:
   • RA (degrees)
   • Dec (degrees)
   • redshift
   • stellar_mass OR log10_stellar_mass
   
   Python check:
   import pandas as pd
   df = pd.read_csv('data.csv')
   required_cols = ['RA', 'Dec', 'redshift', 'stellar_mass']
   missing = [c for c in required_cols if c not in df.columns]
   if missing:
       print(f"Missing columns: {missing}")
   else:
       print("✅ All required columns present")

3. Data Type Check:
   ✅ Numeric columns should be float/int (not strings)
   
   Check:
   df.dtypes  # Print all column types
   df['redshift'].dtype  # Check single column
   
   Fix if needed:
   df['redshift'] = pd.to_numeric(df['redshift'], errors='coerce')
   # errors='coerce' converts bad values to NaN

4. Range Validation:
   RA: 0–360 degrees
   Dec: –90 to +90 degrees
   Redshift: 0–15 (for typical galaxy surveys)
   Stellar mass: 1e6–1e12 M☉ (realistic range)
   
   Python check:
   assert df['RA'].between(0, 360).all(), "RA out of range"
   assert df['Dec'].between(-90, 90).all(), "Dec out of range"
   assert (df['redshift'] >= 0).all(), "Negative redshift"

5. Missing Values (NaN) Check:
   ✅ Some NaN tolerance OK (< 10%)
   ✅ But some columns critical to be complete
   
   Check:
   df.isnull().sum()  # Count NaNs per column
   df.isnull().sum() / len(df)  # Fraction
   
   Example output:
   RA                  0  (0.0% missing)
   Dec                 0  (0.0%)
   redshift            2  (0.1% missing)
   stellar_mass       15  (0.8% missing)
   ← Acceptable
   
   Fix (drop rows with key missing):
   df_clean = df.dropna(subset=['RA', 'Dec', 'redshift', 'stellar_mass'])

6. Duplicate Check:
   ✅ Some duplicates OK
   ✅ But excessive duplication suggests error
   
   Check:
   duplicates = df[df.duplicated(subset=['RA', 'Dec', 'redshift'])]
   print(f"Found {len(duplicates)} duplicates")
   
   Remove duplicates:
   df_unique = df.drop_duplicates(subset=['RA', 'Dec'])

7. Outlier Check:
   ✅ Inspect extreme values
   
   Check:
   df['stellar_mass'].describe()  # Statistics
   # Look at min, max, mean, std
   
   Visualize:
   import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
   df['stellar_mass'].hist(bins=50)
   plt.show()
   # Should see reasonable distribution (not spike at single value)

8. Unit Consistency:
   ✅ Verify all quantities use expected units
   • Magnitudes should be ~18–28 (typical survey range)
   • Stellar mass in M☉ (not g or kg)
   • Wavelength in μm or Å (check header comment)
   
   Fix headers:
   df.columns = [f"{c} [units]" if c not in df.columns else c for c in needed_units]

Full Validation Script:

```python
import pandas as pd
import numpy as np

def validate_cluster_catalog(csv_path):
    """Validate galaxy cluster catalog before upload."""
    
    print(f"Validating {csv_path}...\n")
    
    # Load
    try:
        df = pd.read_csv(csv_path)
    except Exception as e:
        print(f"❌ Failed to load CSV: {e}")
        return False
    
    # Check required columns
    required = ['RA', 'Dec', 'redshift', 'stellar_mass']
    missing = [c for c in required if c not in df.columns]
    if missing:
        print(f"❌ Missing columns: {missing}")
        return False
    print(f"✅ All {len(required)} required columns present")
    
    # Check data types
    try:
        df[required] = df[required].apply(pd.to_numeric, errors='coerce')
    except:
        print("❌ Could not convert columns to numeric")
        return False
    print(f"✅ All columns are numeric")
    
    # Check ranges
    if not df['RA'].between(0, 360).all():
        print(f"⚠️  RA has {(~df['RA'].between(0, 360)).sum()} values out of range")
    if not df['Dec'].between(-90, 90).all():
        print(f"⚠️  Dec has {(~df['Dec'].between(-90, 90)).sum()} values out of range")
    if (df['redshift'] < 0).any():
        print(f"⚠️  {(df['redshift'] < 0).sum()} negative redshifts")
    print(f"✅ Coordinate ranges OK")
    
    # Check NaNs
    nan_fracs = df[required].isnull().sum() / len(df)
    if (nan_fracs > 0.1).any():
        print(f"⚠️  High NaN fraction in: {nan_fracs[nan_fracs > 0.1]}")
    print(f"✅ NaN fraction < 10% in all columns")
    
    # Check duplicates
    n_dupes = df[['RA', 'Dec', 'redshift']].duplicated().sum()
    if n_dupes > 0:
        print(f"⚠️  {n_dupes} duplicate entries ({100*n_dupes/len(df):.1f}% of data)")
    print(f"✅ Duplication level acceptable")
    
    print(f"\n✅ Validation PASSED: {len(df)} galaxies ready for upload!")
    print(f"\nSummary:")
    print(df[required].describe())
    
    return True

# Usage
validate_cluster_catalog('my_catalog.csv')
```

When Validation Fails:
• Check error message for specific issue
• Refer to fix commands above
• If unsure, save cleaned version and re-validate
• Contact support with sample data if validation still fails

Reference: pandas.DataFrame.isnull(), pandas.to_numeric().

faqs.question: What are the version requirements for key dependencies?
faqs.answer:
Dependency Version Specifications:

Core Requirements:
• Python: 3.10 or higher (minimum supported for Streamlit Cloud)
• Streamlit: 1.28+ (latest recommended, auto-installed)
• NumPy: 1.23+ (stable array operations)
• Pandas: 1.3+ (data manipulation)
• SciPy: 1.7+ (scientific computing)
• Matplotlib: 3.5+ (plotting backend)

Astronomy Stack:
• Astropy: 5.1+ (FITS, WCS, units)
• Bagpipes: 2.2+ (SED fitting)
  - Requires: MultiNest, Numpy, Scipy, Astropy
  - Manual install: pip install bagpipes (slow build)
• py21cmfast: 0.3.3+ (21cm simulations, optional)
  - Requires: Fortran compiler (gfortran)
  - Slower installation (~10 min)

Optional Dependencies:
• spaCy: 3.5+ (NLP for FAQ matching, optional)
• h5py: 3.6+ (HDF5 file I/O, optional)
• JWST pipeline: 1.8+ (full pipeline, optional)
  - size: ~500MB, many dependencies

Version Checking:

Check Installed Versions:
```bash
python --version  # Should be 3.10+
pip show numpy | grep Version  # Check individual package
python -c "import numpy; print(numpy.__version__)"
```

Specify Versions in requirements.txt:
```
streamlit==1.32.2
numpy==1.24.3
pandas==2.0.3
scipy==1.11.1
matplotlib==3.8.0
astropy==5.3.2
bagpipes==2.2.5
spacy==3.6.1  # Optional NLP
h5py==3.9.0   # Optional HDF5
```

Python Version Management:

Check current version:
```bash
python --version
```

Switch versions (if multiple installed):
```bash
python3.10 --version  # Specify version
python3.10 -m pip install streamlit  # Install to specific version
```

Upgrade Python (if < 3.10):
• Linux: apt-get install python3.10 (Debian/Ubuntu)
• macOS: brew install python@3.10 (HomeBrew)
• Windows: Download from python.org, run installer

Environment Isolation (Recommended):

Create virtual environment with specific Python:
```bash
python3.10 -m venv venv  # Create venv with Python 3.10
source venv/bin/activate
pip install --upgrade pip setuptools wheel
pip install -r requirements.txt
```

Using Conda (alternative):
```bash
conda create -n astroai python=3.10 scientific_libs
conda activate astroai
pip install streamlit bagpipes
```

Streamlit Cloud Constraints:
• Python version: Determined by requirements.txt runtime.txt
• If not specified: uses 3.9 (default as of 2025)
• Force Python 3.10+:
  • Create runtime.txt in repo root:
    python-3.10.11
  • Commit and push to GitHub
  • Streamlit Cloud detects runtime.txt and uses specified version

Hardware Considerations:

Memory for large dependencies:
• py21cmfast: 2GB+ during install/first run
• JWST pipeline: 3GB+ dependencies
• Bagpipes: 1.5GB+ with Fortran compiler

If install fails on low RAM:
• Install on machine with 8GB+ RAM
• Create wheel (.whl) file: pip wheel . -w ./wheels
• Transfer to target machine and install offline
• Or use smaller requirements-minimal.txt

Breaking Changes:
• NumPy 2.0: Removed some deprecated APIs (avoid if using old code)
• Pandas 2.0: Removed some deprecated APIs
• Astropy 5.0: Updated WCS API
• Recommendation: stay on stable versions listed above

Reference: requirements.txt, INSTALL.md, setup.py.

faqs.question: What does Astro-AI do?
alternative_questions: What analyses are included in Astro-AI?
faqs.answer: Astro-AI is a Streamlit platform that combines three analysis pipelines: cosmic evolution (21 cm simulation), galaxy cluster environment analysis, and JWST spectrum analysis, plus an integrated dashboard and AI-assisted reporting.

faqs.question: Does Astro-AI require all scientific dependencies to run?
alternative_questions: Will the app work without py21cmfast or bagpipes?
faqs.answer: No. The app is designed with fallback simulation paths when optional libraries are missing (for example py21cmfast, bagpipes, JWST pipeline modules). There is also a strict mode option in the sidebar that can enforce required capabilities.

faqs.question: Is there path traversal protection in file I/O utilities?
alternative_questions: How does DataHandler prevent unsafe file paths?
faqs.answer: Yes. DataHandler resolves requested paths under its base data directory and rejects paths that escape that root, helping prevent traversal via filenames like ../.. segments.

faqs.question: How are optional capabilities detected?
alternative_questions: How does strict mode check dependencies?
faqs.answer: The feature flag utility imports known optional modules (such as py21cmfast, tools21cm, bagpipes, jwst, astropy), returns availability status, and can raise a runtime error if required capabilities are missing in strict mode.

faqs.question: How is Bagpipes configured for cloud or read-only environments?
alternative_questions: Why is a temporary bagpipes directory used?
How does setup_bagpipes.py avoid permission issues?
faqs.answer: The project creates a writable temporary bagpipes_data directory in the system temp location (e.g., /tmp on Linux/macOS, C:\Users\*\AppData\Local\Temp on Windows) and sets BAGPIPES_FILTERS and BAGPIPES_DATA to that location. This is done before importing Bagpipes to avoid permission problems in read-only cloud environments.

faqs.question: What does the comparative dashboard integrate?
alternative_questions: How are module results combined in the dashboard?
What is shown in the integration view?
faqs.answer: The dashboard combines status and visual summaries from cosmic evolution, cluster analysis, and JWST spectroscopy, including timeline and comparative plots plus a generated integration report structure.
